objective: Identify the source of MRI signal.
stem: Which property makes hydrogen especially useful for clinical MRI?

options.text: It is abundant in the body and has a nonzero nuclear spin
It is the only element that can absorb radiofrequency energy
Its nucleus contains no electrically charged particles
It produces ionizing radiation when placed in a magnetic field

explanation: Hydrogen is plentiful in water and fat, and its single proton has spin and a magnetic moment. MRI detects radiofrequency signals from those protons rather than ionizing radiation.

stem: Why does tissue develop a small net magnetization parallel to the main magnetic field?

options.text: All protons align parallel and stop precessing
Slightly more protons occupy the lower-energy parallel state
Equal parallel and antiparallel populations rotate in phase
The gradient coils force antiparallel protons out of the tissue

explanation: The parallel state is slightly lower in energy, so it contains a small population excess at thermal equilibrium. Most individual magnetic moments cancel, but that excess produces net longitudinal magnetization.

stem: What happens to the Larmor frequency when the main magnetic field strength increases?

options.text: It decreases in inverse proportion to field strength
It remains fixed for a given tissue
It increases in direct proportion to field strength
It changes only when a gradient is applied

explanation: For a given nucleus, the Larmor frequency equals the gyromagnetic ratio multiplied by magnetic field strength. A stronger B0 therefore makes hydrogen spins precess faster.

stem: Which radiofrequency pulse most efficiently transfers energy to hydrogen protons?

options.text: A pulse applied parallel to B0 at any frequency
A static pulse matched to the gradient switching frequency
A pulse perpendicular to B0 at half the Larmor frequency
A pulse applied perpendicular to B0 at the hydrogen Larmor frequency

explanation: Resonance occurs when the applied RF field matches the spins' Larmor frequency. The transverse B1 field then tips the net magnetization and creates phase coherence.

stem: What directly induces voltage in an MRI receiver coil after excitation?

options.text: Changing transverse magnetization rotating near the coil
Static longitudinal magnetization aligned with B0
The permanent magnetic field of stationary tissue
X-rays emitted as excited protons return to equilibrium

explanation: Precessing transverse magnetization creates a changing magnetic flux through the receiver coil, inducing voltage by Faraday's law. Static longitudinal magnetization does not by itself generate the measured time-varying signal.
